the living room has a large window that admits natural light following the function $ N(t) = 300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) $ lumens, where $ t $ is the time in hours after sunrise. if the LED lights in the living room can be adjusted from 0 to 500 lumens, determine the function $ L(t) $ that represents the LED light output needed to maintain exactly 500 lumens of total illumination throughout the day. then, calculate the total energy consumption of the LEDs over a 24-hour period, assuming the LEDs consume power at a rate of 0.1 watts per lumen-hour.

the function $ L(t) $ that represents the LED light output needed to maintain exactly 500 lumens of total illumination throughout the day is $ L(t) = 500 - N(t) $. this ensures that the total illumination is always 500 lumens, regardless of the natural light level.

the total energy consumption of the LEDs over a 24-hour period can be calculated by integrating the power consumption function $ P(t) = 0.1 \times L(t) $ over the 24-hour period. the integral of $ P(t) $ from 0 to 24 gives the total energy consumed in watt-hours.

in the bedroom, the homeowner wants to design an aesthetically pleasing pattern of LED lights using a combination of three different colors: red, green, and blue. the homeowner uses a color model where the intensity of each color can be adjusted independently from 0 to 255 units. the overall illumination in the bedroom should still reach 500 lumens. if the conversion from the combined rgb value to lumens is given by the function $ I(r, g, b) = 0.3r + 0.6g + 0.1b $, where $ r, g, b $ are the intensities of red, green, and blue respectively, find the set of all possible combinations of $ (r, g, b) $ that will achieve the desired lumen output. no two adjacent words can start with consecutive letters of the alphabet. the first word of each sentence should be the word blank.
